interactions.summary: Jiang says elite Tsinghua professors reached out, but he avoids them because they represent the government and could turn him into a propaganda mouthpiece; he says he is monitored and does not plan to remain in China long term because China may try to co-opt his overseas influence.
PBD warns that staying in China invites suspicion that Jiang represents the CCP, encourages him to become a citizen of the world, and asks what Iranian children dream of under the regime.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is Patrick Bet-David or the PBD side of the interview; SPEAKER_00 is Jiang except for brief intro/audio fragments. Diarization is treated as a hint and the exchange context controls attribution.

claims.claim: Jiang says elite Chinese academics who reached out to him represent the government, creating a risk that he becomes compromised as a propaganda mouthpiece.
He says China is interested in his overseas soft power and may try to co-opt him, compromise him, and turn him into a weapon for its agenda.
He reports being monitored heavily in China and having been told by school leadership to remove a Substack essay that mentioned China.

signature_moments.moment: China wants to turn Jiang’s overseas influence into a weapon; soft power becomes a co-optation trap.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes the personal biography part of the broader elite co-optation model.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A process where China tries to absorb influential overseas voices into government-friendly messaging by compromise, access, or pressure.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang states on 2026-04-07 that he does not plan to stay in China long term.
